questions.question: Explain backward chaining with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Backward chaining starts from goal/hypothesis and works backwards
Goal-driven inference technique
Example: To prove 'Patient has Fever', check rules: Fever ← HighTemp ∧ Infection, then check if patient has HighTemp and Infection using known facts
Uses depth-first search and backtracking
Common in expert systems like medical diagnosis

questions.question: Differentiate between forward chaining and backward chaining.
keyPoints: Forward chaining: data-driven, starts from facts → conclusions
Backward chaining: goal-driven, starts from goal → required facts
Forward: bottom-up, Backward: top-down approach
Forward better for monitoring, backward for diagnosis
Forward explores all possibilities, backward focuses on specific goals

questions.question: What is backtracking in backward chaining? Explain its importance.
keyPoints: Backtracking returns to previous choice points when a rule path fails
Essential for exploring alternative solution paths
Prevents getting stuck on failed branches
Allows system to find multiple solutions
Without backtracking, only first found solution would be returned

questions.question: Explain the concept of Horn clauses in logic programming.
keyPoints: Horn clause has at most one positive literal
Form: A ← B1, B2, ..., Bn or Head :- Body
Used extensively in Prolog and backward chaining
Allows efficient reasoning with definite clauses
Multiple conditions (body) lead to single conclusion (head)

questions.question: What is a proof tree in backward chaining? How is it constructed?
keyPoints: Proof tree shows hierarchical goal-subgoal relationships
Root node = main goal to prove
Intermediate nodes = sub-goals derived from rules
Leaf nodes = known facts in database
Constructed by recursive rule application until facts are reached

questions.question: Describe the role of unification in backward chaining.
keyPoints: Unification makes two atomic formulas identical via variable bindings
Essential for matching goal with rule heads and facts
Substitutions are propagated to sub-goals
If unification fails, backtrack to try alternative rules
Handles pattern matching between goals and knowledge base

questions.question: List and explain advantages and disadvantages of backward chaining.
keyPoints: Advantages: Efficient for few goals, focuses on relevant rules, good for diagnostics, explainable reasoning
Disadvantages: May explore irrelevant paths, inefficient with many goals, can loop infinitely, may miss all solutions
Best suited for consultation/diagnostic expert systems
Used in Prolog, Mycin and similar systems

questions.question: Explain backward chaining in detail. How does it work? Discuss its algorithm and applications in expert systems.
keyPoints: Definition: Goal-driven inference starting from hypothesis → facts
Algorithm steps: 1) Select goal 2) Find rules with goal in head 3) Match facts 4) Prove sub-goals recursively 5) Backtrack on failure
Uses depth-first search, goal stack, unification, backtracking
Proof tree construction
Applications: Medical diagnosis (Mycin), Troubleshooting systems, Consultation systems
Prolog implementation details
Comparison with forward chaining

questions.question: Compare forward chaining and backward chaining. Under what circumstances is each approach preferred?
keyPoints: Forward chaining: data-driven, starts from facts, explores all rules, exhaustive
Backward chaining: goal-driven, starts from goal, focuses on relevant rules
When to use forward: monitoring, control systems, when all data available
When to use backward: diagnosis, troubleshooting, when goals are limited
Efficiency comparison: forward may do unnecessary work, backward focused
Hybrid approaches combining both
Examples: Forward in production systems, backward in Prolog/Mycin

questions.question: Explain how Prolog implements backward chaining. Discuss the role of depth-first search, backtracking, and unification.
keyPoints: Prolog uses SLD-resolution with backward chaining
Goal stack implemented as LIFO structure
Depth-first search: pursues one branch completely before alternatives
Backtracking: returns to choice points on failure, undoes variable bindings
Unification: matches goals with facts/rules, creates variable bindings
Cut operator (!) controls search space
Demonstrate with example program trace
Explain how proof is constructed via resolution

questions.question: Design an expert system for medical diagnosis using backward chaining. Explain with rules, facts, and proof tree.
keyPoints: Define sample knowledge base with disease rules
Example: Flu ← Fever ∧ Cough ∧ Fatigue
Facts: Patient has Fever, Cough, Fatigue
Goal: Prove Patient has Flu
Construct proof tree showing goal → sub-goals → facts
Explain backtracking for multiple diseases
Show how system would query for missing information
Discuss limitations and improvements
